column1: # Named EtherCAT/IO bindings into the machine registry.
# M-code → digital-output bindings. Slave/bit are placeholders that match a
# generic 16-channel DO module; operator overrides via the TSV when wiring a
# real coolant/clamp manifold. If the binding doesn't resolve to a real slot
# the registry silently no-ops the write (per signals.cpp), so an unedited
# build still parses M3/M7/M8/M62 without faulting.
# LinuxCNC M62/M63/M64/M65 P-word digital outputs. Indexed 0..7.
# Richer field-level PDO / drive bindings for heterogeneous EtherCAT nodes.
# Manual pulse generator (handwheel). 32-bit signed cumulative quadrature
# counter at byte offset 0 of slave 10's TxPDO slot. Slave 10 is a
# placeholder — operator overrides via `mpg bind` or wire_mpg_from_signal
# resolves at runtime when a real handwheel is attached.
# Hardware safety inputs on a dedicated 16-channel DI slave. Slave 11 is a
# placeholder — operator overrides via TSV when wiring a real safety relay
# module (typical: EL1904 TwinSAFE, ESI-described SDI module, or a generic
# EL1809). Until then the registry silently skips the slave read so the
# symbols stay at their default of `false` and motion::cycle_safety treats
# WIRING CONVENTION: every safety input is treated as ACTIVE-HIGH at the
# kernel boundary — value=1 means "asserted / unsafe condition". Real CNC
# practice wires E-stop and door interlocks NORMALLY-CLOSED so a broken
# wire trips the safety path; the field-side adapter (or the safety relay
# module's own NC->NO inversion) MUST present an active-high bit on the
# EtherCAT PDO so the kernel logic stays vendor-neutral.
